title: Activation
description: The numeric output of a layer after its linear transform and nonlinearity, passed forward through the network.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: After a layer applies its weights to an input tensor, the result (often after GELU, ReLU, or another nonlinearity) is that layer’s activation. In a transformer block, the post-attention and post-MLP tensors are typical activation examples.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Activations determine what information reaches the next layer and what backpropagation will differentiate. Memory planners track activation tensors during training because they are large but temporary, unlike checkpointed parameters. This page is the broad foundation for that idea; pages like ReLU, LeakyReLU, and SiLU zoom in on specific FFN activation choices, while SwiGLU shows how gating can turn that idea into a different FFN block shape.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: For a batch of one sequence, a hidden layer might output a tensor of shape (sequence_length, hidden_size). That tensor is the activation handed to the next sublayer.

whereItAppears.title: Where It Appears
whereItAppears.body: Profiling tools chart activation magnitudes per layer. Interpretability work sometimes inspects specific activation directions. In inference, activations are usually recomputed on each forward pass rather than saved in the weight file.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: A hidden activation is not the same as softmax: softmax turns vocabulary logits into a probability vector at the output head, while activations are internal layer outputs that may never be normalized across the vocabulary. Saying a “ReLU activation” refers to one specific nonlinearity that shapes FFN activations, not to the softmax step at decode time. ReLU, LeakyReLU, and SiLU are specific activation choices; SwiGLU goes one step further and changes the FFN into a gated two-branch block.

readerShortcut.title: Reader Shortcut
readerShortcut.body: Parameters are the stored weights; activations are the live values those weights produce for your prompt as it moves through the stack.

openingSummary: An activation is the tensor a layer outputs for a given input—hidden states in transformers are intermediate signals flowing between blocks until the vocabulary head produces logits, distinct from final softmax probabilities.
